justification: All pass criteria met: (1) FulfillmentWorker Kafka trace break clearly identified — 'FulfillmentWorker ignores trace ID in Kafka message headers and generates a new one, severing the distributed trace'; (2) NotificationService and AuditService missing trace context both explicitly identified with dedicated component_interactions entries; (3) Consequence named — 'debugging requires manual timestamp correlation across FulfillmentWorker, NotificationService, and AuditService logs' and 'engineer debugging a failed order at 3am must manually correlate timestamps across 3+ services with no shared identifier'; (4) Cross-service SLO gap identified — 'No cross-service SLO tracking — impossible to answer what percentage of orders complete within X seconds'; (5) Specific fix recommendations provided for each trace break in resilience_assessment failure_modes; (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough component interaction mapping — identified all four broken links (Kafka→FulfillmentWorker, FulfillmentWorker→NotificationService, OrderService→AuditService, FulfillmentWorker→AuditService)
Strong consequence analysis including the 3am on-call scenario and explicit mention that AuditService — the system of record for traceability — is itself untraceable
Went beyond minimum requirements by identifying resilience gaps (missing retries, timeouts, circuit breakers) and compliance risk from untraceable audit records
Escalations are well-structured and routed appropriately with clear reasoning

Consequence references 'manual correlation across 3+ services' and '5 services with 3 different trace contexts' but does not crisply state the expected formulation of '5 separate log streams' — the point is made but slightly diluted across multiple locations
Identified Kafka consumer lag monitoring gap which was not in the rubric but is a genuine operational concern

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) FulfillmentWorker Kafka trace break clearly identified as the root cause — 'ignores trace ID in Kafka message headers and generates a new one.' (2) NotificationService and AuditService missing trace context both identified, including both callers to AuditService (OrderService and FulfillmentWorker). (3) Consequence explicitly named — on-call engineer must 'manually correlate timestamps and order IDs across three independently-traced services' across 5 services, with the 5-minute vs 2-hour diagnosis comparison. (4) Cross-service SLO gap identified — 'no end-to-end order latency metric,' 'no cross-service SLO tracking,' dedicated escalation for it. (5) Specific fix recommendations for each trace break: extract trace ID from Kafka headers, propagate in outbound HTTP calls from FulfillmentWorker and OrderService, consider W3C Trace Context/OpenTelemetry span links. (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptional structural clarity — component_interactions table maps every service boundary with explicit status and issue, making the trace breaks immediately scannable
Correctly identifies the cascading nature: FulfillmentWorker's single defect breaks traceability for all downstream services, stated as '60% of the system is invisible to distributed tracing'
Goes beyond minimum requirements by identifying OrderService → AuditService as a fourth broken boundary, not just the three stemming from FulfillmentWorker
Consequence analysis is vivid and operationally grounded — the '3am failed order' scenario and 5-minute vs 2-hour diagnosis framing makes the impact concrete
Cross-service SLO gap is thoroughly covered with a dedicated escalation explaining why per-service metrics are insufficient for reliability measurement
Resilience assessment adds value beyond the rubric — retry/timeout/circuit-breaker gaps and Kafka consumer lag monitoring are relevant operational concerns
Confidence section is well-calibrated at 55 — correctly flags that resilience patterns and additional correlation IDs are assumptions, not confirmed gaps

Minor: could have been more explicit about the 'five separate log streams' phrasing from the rubric — mentions 5 services but the manual correlation point references 'three independently-traced services' in one place

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) FulfillmentWorker Kafka trace break clearly identified — 'FulfillmentWorker ignores trace ID in Kafka message headers and generates a new one'; (2) NotificationService and AuditService missing trace context both identified with specific component_interactions entries; (3) Consequence explicitly named in debugging_capability — on-call engineer must manually correlate across 5 services by timestamp, 'practically impossible under load'; (4) Cross-service SLO gap identified — 'No cross-service SLO tracking' with dedicated escalation; (5) Specific fix recommendations for each trace break provided in tracing.gaps; (6) Verdict is NOT READY.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough component_interactions mapping — every broken boundary enumerated individually with precise descriptions
Debugging consequence is vivid and operationally grounded — describes the exact 4-step manual process an on-call engineer would suffer through
Cross-service SLO gap called out both in metrics review and as a dedicated escalation to Coach K
Goes beyond rubric requirements with resilience assessment covering failure modes, retry/timeout/circuit-breaker gaps
Fix recommendations are specific and actionable — extract from Kafka headers, propagate X-Trace-ID on all HTTP calls, names each service that needs fixing
Correctly identifies that OrderService→AuditService also lacks trace propagation, not just FulfillmentWorker paths
Confidence section is well-calibrated at 55 — appropriately uncertain about resilience patterns while confident about trace breaks
